comments.content:
[IMAGE:0] It's almost midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a bit of alchemical text: 
 
"It [the first matter] has been compared to all things in the world, visible and invisible, possessed of a soul or not, corporeal or spiritual, dead or alive, mineral or vegetable; to the elements and their compositions, to things hot and cold, further to all colors, all fruits, all birds, and in short to all things between heaven and earth." 
 
The image is the third illustration from Splendor Solis, the most gorgeously illustrated of all the old alchemical texts. The text on the shield says, "From two waters make one, which you will seek to make into Sun and Moon, and make them drink the inimical wine.  You will see when they are dead.  Then make earth out of water and you will multiply the stone."

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed.  See you next week!***

[IMAGE:1]

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

comments.title: Rulerships
comments.content:
I considered the following rulerships after reading your delineation of the upcoming Presidential inauguration. I was struck by your treatment of the rulerships of the Houses according to their Zodiacal placements. I hadn't realized that this could have such an influence; so I again reviewed my natal chart with this in mind, and the foregoing questions presented themselves.

My natal Saturn rules my Second House, and is posited in the angular First House. He has some afflictions, but is basically strong in his domicile Capricorn. Since I gather that the Second House that he rules is a House of earned income, does this mean that I have a chance of earning through Saturn-related occupations?

Likewise my natal Sun, also well placed, rules the Ninth. I have read that besides philosophy and religion and all the deep questions whose answer is 42, the 9th is connected with publishing. Since the Sun is to some extent a wealth planet, does this mean I have a shot at earning by authorship or publishing, or is the 9th nothing to do with that?

I ask because my natal Jupiter and the ruler of my midheaven are afflicted, by each other, and weak in cadent Houses, making their situations a source of lifelong frustration.

I would like to get a big book such as Llewellyn George's A-Z Delineator and work all this out myself; and indeed last year I *did* get that book, through ILL, and took much time to make a thorough delineation of my chart. And Mr. George told me that by a certain placement I have much difficulty acquiring the books I need in order to learn things: and experience has shown this to be so.

replies.title: Re: Rulerships
replies.content: It's a source of puzzlement to me that so many modern astrologers neglect the practical implications of planetary house rulerships.  If your natal Saturn is strongly placed and rules the 2nd, that means that you'll have to struggle financially in youth but will end up quite comfortable by the time you get old. You will have to work for it -- Saturn is always a planet of hard work, and especially in this context -- but you can prosper through your own efforts. How to do so is something you'll have to read based on Saturn's aspects and other indications in the chart. As for the 9th, it can tell you whether you can get published -- that'll depend as much on the Sun's aspects and the house in which it's located as anything -- but it can't tell you whether you can make money at it.

replies.title: Re: Rulerships
replies.content:
Thanks! I'll be applying this. There is a fly in the ointment: my Saturn is opposed by a seriously afflicted Mars, and is retrograde. L. George foretells all kinds of bad things from this; but then I've already encountered plenty of reversals, opposition, obstacles and difficulty, so why worry about them at this late date?

My Sun is well favored, so it looks pretty good for getting published. Also, I think the Part of Fortune is in the House he rules; I'll have to review the method for locating it.

I mean to get George's book (the first edition - I've only ever seen the second) when I can, and at least one of the books on transits previously mentioned here. I'll watch closely for mention of any other important reference texts on the topic.

comments.title: Basic Magical Practices
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid

I have been doing discursive meditation for a short while (with some breaks). I have just bought a set of tarot cards to begin divination. The next step once I have the first two in place as habits is beginning the SOP in the next couple of months.

As I understand it, these are the three basic magical practices. The idea is to do these alone for a few years (plus reading) till my kids are older and I have more time to really look into ritual magic.

I have seen you post several times that once you start down the path of magic, you can't stop and you pretty much have to continue at least basic ritual practices for the rest of your life as a protective measure, because you become sensitized to the other planes. Is that an accurate summary?

Will discursive meditation+divination+SOP take me to that level or do I need to get into proper ritual magic for that? Or can I stop them safely at any time if I'm only doing those three things? Will cutting out the SOP make a difference?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content:
The basic triad of magical practices are right on the edge. Some people can do those for a while, stop, and go do something different with their lives. Others find that they're across the border:

"For mark! no sooner was I fairly found
Pledged to the plain, after a pace or two,
Than, pausing to throw backward a last view
O'er the safe road, 'twas gone; gray plain all round:
Nothing but plain to the horizon's bound,
I might go on; naught else remained to do."

If you aren't ready to commit to the path, you can practice a little meditation, a little divination, do some reading here and there, and be fine. Serious practice of the three elements of the basic triad, and you're in the gray zone.

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content: I'm sure this has been asked before, but out of curiosity, how do you know when you've crossed the border?

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content: If you stop the practices and things start feeling weird and creepy, and don't stop feeling that way until you start the practices again. What's happened is that you've awakened your astral senses far enough that you can feel what unclean astral space feels like.

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content: Can it go further than being uncomfortable at astral gunk? That sounds like something that could turn into madness if advanced enough.

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content: I've never heard of any case going that far.

replies.title: Re: Basic Magical Practices
replies.content:
I can offer a fair case-study here. I was fully, daily, engaged in a solid well-rounded daily practice for a full year before switching systems. Things were improving pretty steadily the whole time. Since then - about 5 months now - I haven't practiced what I consider to be a complete ritual. I've just been doing an Elemental Cross and circulating the light in the DA system - Grade 1 stuff. I've fallen almost completely off of discursive meditation, and given up divination temporarily. I didn't feel like I was getting good answers.

But I think in that time I've gotten deep enough that I have to return to my full routine. Things have been really weird lately. Pot has been doing its best to get back onstage. And every time I give in, something not-altogether-pleasant happens. The message seems pretty clear really..

comments.title: How does one tell what is a magic system?
comments.content:
In 2013 I decided to try out feng shui. I got a book on western feng shui, which orients the guas to the front door rather than to the compass like traditional (proper?) feng shui does. That year I had more work than before or since (and fell in love harder and more painfully than for a long time before or since). I also burnt out by the end of 2014 and still haven't recovered nor would I ever want to repeat the experience of burning out on several fronts like that.

In January 2014 I decided to try traditional feng shui but tripped over how complicated it all seemed, so ended up stuck in indecision about the two systems. I felt I had monkey-wrenched it for myself, unwilling to go back to the 'fake' western version and unable to understand the genuine system.

I am now wondering if feng shui is a form of magic system. You said it is a bad idea to mix two systems if you don't know what you're doing. So my questions is, how does one even know when one is dealing with magic systems? I'd like to avoid inadvertently throwing a spanner in the works in future just because I didn't realise I was working with a magic system and was in the process of switching to another magic system.

replies.title: Re: How does one tell what is a magic system?
replies.content:
1) There are many different schools of feng shui. One of the differences among them is that some orient toward physical surroundings and others orient toward compass points. The first one you used isn't "fake," it just belongs to the end of feng shui that focuses on physical surroundings. You can find very detailed accounts of traditonal lore from both systems.

2) Feng shui isn't a magical system as such, but it's powerful stuff -- as you found out! As for figuring out what might be worth treating as a magical system, one thing I use is what I call the James Randi Criterion: anything that a rabid dogmatic materialist like the late James Randi would dismiss as "woo-woo" should be handled with the care appropriate to something that might involve magic. Put more simply, if it scares a rationalist, treat it as magic.

replies.title: Re: Daily astrology question
replies.content: It's simpler than that, and uses the ephemeris rather than a daily chart. I compare the positions of the planets over the course of the day with the planets in each of our natal charts. The Moon moves fast enough that it will often make several aspects in the course of the day, and they can be roughly timed with a little quick math: if the Moon passes from 6° to 18° Cancer in the course of the day, for example, and you happen to have Mercury at 12° Pisces, you know that between 10 am and noon, roughly, is a good time for any Mercurial activity. Mercury at its fastest moves through two degrees in a day; the others are much slower, so aspects last for a couple of days or more. So it's not a snapshot, it's a rough schedule:  today is fortunate for your Venus but your natal Mars is afflicted, so be extra careful crossing the street, and so on.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can get the same results from daydreams as from dreams. Jung's famous book Symbols of Transformation, the book that marked his break with Freud and began his public exploration of the world of myth and symbol, is an interpretation of a series of daydreams and fantasies, not nocturnal dreams. The systematic use of daydreaming as a tool for psychological exploration has been developed in quite a bit of detail -- "active imagination" is the polite term for it -- and it's also much used in magical circles, where it's called "scrying" or "pathworking."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, let's start with some basics. According to Dion Fortune, who (as you might know) was one of the 20th century's great mages, magic is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. Do you believe it's possible to cause changes in consciousness in accordance with will? Then you believe in magic. Harry Potter special-effects crap is not magic. That's the stuff the establishment uses to distract you from actual magic.

Second, you can read all the books you like and not get any closer to magic. If you want to find out whether magic works, plan on spending a year or so of daily practice, around a half hour a day, to wake up your dulled and atrophied inner senses to the point that you can notice what's been going on around you all along.

Third, drugs are a crutch. Using drugs to make magical states of consciousness easier to get to is like trying to build strength by lifting weights made of styrofoam. If you actually want to get strong, you have put up with the inconvenience of pumping iron instead!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Esoteric Christian traditions reject the apocalyptic reading of history and the notion that there's only one life as misunderstandings that found their way into Christianity after the church hierarchy turned against the inner, esoteric tradition (labeling them Gnostic heretics). From within the traditions I'm familiar with, there is a Kingdom of God, as Jesus taught, and it's not found by shouting "Lo, here!" and "Lo, there!" as the believers in imminent apocalypse do; as the man said, it is within you -- a state of consciousness. There is a heaven, and it's the state to which you rise when you've finished your work at the human level.

Faith, by the way, is not the same thing as belief; you can believe this or that theological claim about Jesus until you're blue in the face, and not undergo the metanoia, the baptism of the Holy Spirit that lifts you out of your current state into a higher mode of being. Faith is trust in God, and it's one step in the process of spiritual work that brings you to the Kingdom of God.

That's the teaching as I was taught it by the Christian occultists I respect most. Make of it what you will.

comments.title: Some meditation, divination, and ritual questions.

comments.title: Here Is Your Scarab
comments.content:
I meant to have posted this last week, but I didn't get it written out in time. At any rate, here's some TSW for everybody's enjoyment.


My wife and I have since June been trying to find a house, ever since we found out that she was pregnant with our first child. Our living situation for the past several years has been pretty awful: to put it plainly, we've been confined by circumstance to the semi-finished but somewhat moldy basement of my mother-in-law, who's inclined towards hoarding unsustainable quantities of chihuahuas and not letting them outside to use the bathroom or cleaning up very well after them. Our space is as sealed off and as clean as possible, and we try to help out as much as we can, but we still have to go upstairs to shower, and the poor air quality filters down to us. In short, it's not been pleasant.

I've completely lost track of the number of houses we've looked at this year. We put down offers on four of them, each of which were perfect for us in different ways; each time, our (increasingly competitive) offers were rejected in favor of somebody else's. We were starting to get desperate, as a pregnancy is hard enough to go through in that environment and we absolutely refused, if it could at all be helped, to ever bring our baby into that house.

A couple months or so ago, I remember seeing you recommend planetary charity to Saturn as the Lord of Real Estate in order to solve a similar problem with difficulty, and so I resolved towards performing the working of planetary Saturnian charity you prescribed over the course of the next three weeks. I even drew quite positive cards beforehand, indicating, as I thought, that this would be a successful, positive working. However, I foolishly neglected to think about Saturn's apparently awkward position in my natal chart for this sort of work--retrograde, in the Fourth House of real estate, and in direct opposition to Mars. Suffice it to say, that working did not (ahem) work--at least not directly. What happened instead was that the candle I was burning behaved as no other candle working I've ever done has done, and proceeded to suddenly engulf my altar and the entire corner of the room in which it stands in a blazing fire, filling the entire room with smoke before I managed to put it out. Here's the miraculous bit: While the altar cloths were consumed, not a single other item on my altar was in the least bit damaged, nor was the bookshelf immediately next to it even touched by the impressively large flames. Literally the most significant thing that happened was that my statue of the Morrígan, which had taken a primary position on the altar until then, was knocked to the floor. I am a polytheist Christian Druid, and my immediate impression (once I'd gotten over the initial shock of a small and sudden house fire), afterwards confirmed by divination, was that the altar was set up in a dissatisfactory way so as to be unconducive to what the Gods deem should be my priorities, which is that Christ should occupy the central role, regardless of whatever other Powers are at work in my life (as they are of course welcome to be). I consulted with the Morrígan as well, who confirmed that while She wasn't going anywhere overall, She would be withdrawing because Her energies "are not what I need right now." Which is pretty typical of my relationship with Her; she flits in and out of observability in my life while directing many of my courses whether I'm aware of Her presence or not. The fire itself, though, reminded of nothing so much as the flame through which God first speaks to Moses on the mountain, which engulfed but did not consume the bush.

Meanwhile, we continued to look at houses, each more disappointing than the last, and the inventory of available houses coming on the market (already dismally low this year thanks to the pandemic) was beginning to slow to a trickle as the holiday season crept closer--as well as my wife's due date. Consulting with a friend about my thoughts above regarding the Saturn working, and my impressions that perhaps I was being directed to turn in a more Christian-oriented direction regarding this issue, he suggested maybe I take it through "official channels" and actually visit a Catholic church where they have active shrines with votive candles to light. I actually managed to find one nearby that was holding services and did just that. Meanwhile, I also decided to start a novena to Our Lady of Loreto, who I'd very recently discovered through looking around on the Lucky Mojo and esoteric Catholic fora is associated with house-finding, lighting a dressed candle to her daily (which all burned very safely!), and praying that she would help us find a home quickly, safe, affordable, in good repair, and that we would be approved for it, in return for which I would give money (which I was setting aside daily) to the poor and homeless, make her work known to all my friends, and consecrate our home to her. Now for the really weird TSW moment: On day eight of the novena, we were scheduled to go look at yet another house. That morning, almost on a whim, I did an internet search for Our Lady of Loreto, hoping to find an icon of her to place in whatever house we did end up. What should I find instead however but a convent dedicated to her smack dab in the middle of our little town, tucked around a corner that I pass every single day in complete ignorance! That seemed far too great a coincidence (as it is called) to ignore, and so, after we went to look at the house--which we both instantly fell in complete love with, which ticked off every box we'd wanted, and which is only five minutes from my work, I contacted the convent to explain our situation and ask for prayers. The sisters seemed equal parts surprised and delighted, and said that they would of course pray for us. The next morning, the ninth and final day of the novena, we got the news that our offer was accepted.

The punch line? After nine days of Hail Marys...the house provided for us by the Queen of Heaven ended up being located on 9th Ave., in a humble little town that, were I to name it would be far too locatable with a quick Google search but to which the Blessed Virgin might as well have signed her name. I've never heard of St. Mary being snarky, but I'm certainly beginning to get that impression!

replies.title: Re: Here Is Your Scarab
replies.content: Thanks for this! I've heard more than one esoteric Christian say that the Blessed Virgin is, if not snarky, at least equipped with the sense of humor you'd expect in someone who's been dealing with the endless domestic troubles of her daughter-in-law (the Church, after all, is the bride of Christ) for two thousand years...

replies.title: Re: Mr Dee
replies.content:
That's Dr. Dee, to start with -- he was an extraordinarly educated, intelligent, and competent man, an expert mathematician, astrologer, navigator, and mage. The great sailing captains of the Elizabethan era consulted with him on issues of maps and navigation charts, and he wrote a preface to the first English translation of Euclid's book on geometry that inspired countless people to get into mathematics over the following century. Was he fooled by a two-bit con artist? I find that very, very hard to believe.

The proof of the pudding, however, is in the eating. Since Dee's time, many occultists have used the material he and Kelly got from the spirits to good effect. Enochian magic, the system of magic that began with Dee and Kelly, is still quite extensively practiced today. (I've worked with it myself.) That shows me that there are indeed spiritual beings involved in the system...

comments.title: Metaphysical miscellany
comments.content:
Why does “goodness” tend to yield such perverse results as opposed to selfish actions? I have met many happy evil people and many miserable good ones.

In a similar vein, why is the Christian god so cruel to its followers? I can nearly recognize a Christian by their outward poverty and general misery. I have not observed this in other religions. Indeed some seem to bestow prosperity and blessings.

Thanks for your insight, and anyone else feel free to chime in. I was raised Christian and cannot seem to settle In or out.

replies.title: Re: Metaphysical miscellany

replies.title: Re: Metaphysical miscellany
replies.content: It all depends on your definition of goodness. A great many Christians these days have a definition of goodness that includes being victimized as a sign of virtue, and so they get victimized. A great many also have a definition of goodness that includes repressing every thought and feeling that isn't considered virtuous, and so they end up as psychological basket cases seething with repressed feelings and cravings -- another very good way to be miserable. Not all branches of Christianity have an understanding of goodness that produces those wretched results, but too many do.

replies.title: Christian definitions of "good"
replies.content:
Some Christians have a definition of good that depends on supporting some political stance, such as Prohibition, opposing capital punishment, opposing abortion, etc. Without arguing the merits of any of these causes I would note that they can provide a rather shallow definition of good. For example, making X your acid test of Christianity is very easy, it doesn't require you to spend a lot of time as visiting the sick or prisoners would; it doesn't require you to give deeply of your own resources, such as regular contributions to the food bank; it doesn't require you to give up a vice, such as smoking or drinking. All you have to do is vote for politicians who mouth the party line on X and there you are, a guardian of the Good. Pretty sweet deal. Of course this type of behavior leaves those who practice it self satisfied, not miserable and feeling oppressed.

This tendency is not exclusive to Christians of course. Our esteemed host has frequently pointed out the type of person who considers that a donation to Greenpeace and a vote for the candidate who mouths the appropriate words about climate change marks them as a friend of the Earth, despite the mileage on their SUV and home full of electronics.

Rita

comments.title: Cartomancy
comments.content:
There is a book on cartomancy called "Fifty-four Devils". I want to read it but I don't want to go to Hell or end up in other bad situations due to.. well I don't know, because I am a beginner and I don't know what can happen. But from what I've read doing bad magic is bad for you as the practitioner too.

The description of the book is as follows:
"""
“Ye are twenty-five cards.
Become twenty-five devils
Enter into the body, into the blood, into the soul .”
So begins a nineteenth century Italian charm making use of a small deck of playing cards. This brief-but-richly drawn book explores the practice of divination by playing cards—known as cartomancy. It reveals the “secrets in plain sight” which hide within the pips, kings, queens, and jokers of a standard deck. Explore one method of divination in-depth as you meet the “fifty-four devils”—the symbolic spirits of each card—and learn about invoking ancestral blessings for card readings, the folklore of playing cards, and how to relate fairy tales to a spread along the way.
"""

Do you think this is magically risky? Are the "devils" here forces of evil or benign spirits?

replies.title: Re: Cartomancy
replies.content: I'd have to read the book to know whether it involves messing with malevolent spirits or not, and frankly, with that kind of back cover blurb, it's not the kiind of book I'd want to read. There are plenty of other good books on cartomancy that don't have that problem!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Sorry, I just asked the question about cartomancy and whether a certain kind of magic is evil and/or risky, and forgot this addendum. Is witchcraft [magic that calls itself witchcraft] in general evil or risky? Or is it just a kind of magic that has gotten a bad rap?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really depends. The word "witchcraft" has been picked up and used by a very diverse range of magical and Pagan religious traditions during the last three quarters of a century. Some of them are totally benign, some of them are corrupt and evil, some are all over the space in between. The mere use of the brand name "witchcraft" basically tells you nothing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is there a way I can tell whether some magic is corrupt / evil / involving malevolent spirits or not?

Since I am a beginner I am a bit scared of the risk. I suppose I can stick to some specific book that I know to be safe but I'm curious and I want to explore, without ending up doing bad things or becoming evil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd recommend starting with a safe book that will teach you protective rituals and the like. Then, when you've learned that, you can do your exploration and not be at any risk. As far as doing bad things or becoming evil, that's not something a book or a system of magic will do to you -- the evil in evil magic comes from the evil intentions of those who practice it, and if you always check everything against your conscience and your sense of right and wrong, you should be fine.

comments.title: Second spiritual awakening
comments.content:
Brother Greer,

1) what is your preferred name to address you by? I see many use JMG, but I thought I might ask you directly.

2) JMG and others: recently, in the past few days, I've been having what I feel to be some sort of heightened mental state, with a burst of creative and intellectual energy that feels like 5 or 10 times sharper and more observant than usual, and epiphanies and synchronicities coming fast and strong. Almost like I'm entering a form of discursive meditation, but the processing of info is rapid and takes very little work. I've been reading a lot of the feast for thought from your other blog and some of the links posted in the recent comments, and it is inspiring me to make wonderful connections between ideas, almost as if I am playing the Glass Bead Game. None of this feels scary or overwhelming; it's quite pleasant and exciting. I am sketching down most of the broad brush strokes of my ideas as they come to me, in the hope that I will have the time and energy to return to them and flesh them out further. I've even thought of the beginnings of a short story/novella about two characters and their differing responses to the Great Reset based on their own socio-economic and subcultural backgrounds.
This all comes with an overall feeling of serenity, a sense of a higher benevolence helping and guiding me, deep gratitude, and the desire to use this state and the insights it provides for the good of others and the planet.
I have some theories on mundane causes for this state, including the loss of my sense of taste and smell after getting the 'rona a month ago. I'm also working to remind myself to rest, exercise, and eat well (esp. including some meat) to help ground myself and not lose touch with my body. I'm aware that some of the things I'm describing could be attributed to a manic state, but I've never had any mental illness, nor does it run in my family.
There's been one other time in my life that I felt this way, what I call my "spiritual awakening," a part of my life that convinced me of higher powers than human minds that we can access, apparently sometimes spontaneously.
What are your and your reader's thoughts? Is this a sign that I should start a regular spiritual or magical practice? A nudge from the gods in some way? This has been a momentous year fir astrology--could there be a connection there?

replies.title: Re: Second spiritual awakening
replies.content:
1) I normally go by JMG online, but thanks for asking.

2) Let the experience guide you. Some people just have this sort of thing happen -- I suspect it's the result of extensive spiritual practice in previous lives. If you're meant to take up spiritual or magical practice, you'll be guided to what you need to do.

replies.title: Re: Second spiritual awakening
replies.content:
Thank you! In both instances, I've felt that I've lived here before.

--D.G. Martin

comments.title: Rapid image cycling
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Are you aware of any system of magic that makes use of rapid image cycling?

I read William Bengston's The Energy Cure. Bengston is a professor of sociology who, during his teens, stumbled across a home-grown psychic who could perform hands-on healings among other feats. Together, they developed a method of cycling through images of various things one desires until one feels the "flow" of something. The energy is channeled to heal and Bengston has had some very interesting experiments on mice and cells in addition to healing humans around himself.

Bengston sees himself as a true skeptic rather than the true believers in materialist orthodoxy. You have a lot on your plate but you might like to check out his site bengstonresearch.com or his book if you have time.

Anyway, regarding the question, I was just curious whether you know about similar methods. As far as I personally know, and from trying to go through New Thought literature, visualizations of desired outcomes were practised over the course of New Thought and probably date back to Mesmerism. However they use just one detailed image that might be held in the mind during an altered state. By contrast, this rapid cycling method seems designed to trigger etheric flow?

replies.title: Re: Rapid image cycling
replies.content: As far as I know this is a new thing. Certainly I don't recall anything else like it in the older literature.

comments.title: The Etheric Plane, The Astral Plane, and the Heaviside Layer
comments.content: Being a cat person and an Eliot fan, are you familiar with Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats and/or the musical Cats which is based on it? Not sure it's your taste music-wise, but they way they expanded the original book with material from "Rhapsody on a Windy Night" and "The Dry Salvages" as well as other unpublished poems by Eliot, well, it seems to this budding speculative mage that the show has quite a bit of occult philosophy subtlety stuck here and there. Granted it's no "Waste Land" but IMO at does reflect that Eliot knew what he was about esoterically. Read or seen it? Thoughts?

replies.title: Re: The Etheric Plane, The Astral Plane, and the Heaviside Layer
replies.content: I've read the book, but never seen the musical -- I'm not a great fan of those. Eliot was pretty orthodox in his Christianity but he reached his poetic maturity in a literary culture that was saturated with magic and Asian philosophy, so there was some serious spillover. I find The Waste Land one of the few really great Moderninst poems, though Four Quartets is more to my taste.

replies.title: Re: The Etheric Plane, The Astral Plane, and the Heaviside Layer
replies.content:
I find the themes of transcendence and personal transformation in the show to be downright alchemical at times. I'm sure the libretto is available online somewhere if you ever get curious (and run out of things to do, HA!)

I've found that even orthodox Christianity, if it's sufficiently sacramental, often gets at quite a bit of esotericism in spite of itself.

replies.title: Re: The Etheric Plane, The Astral Plane, and the Heaviside Layer
replies.content: Sacramental Christianity is esoteric through and through. It's been the frustration and burden of generations of Christian esotericists that so many people, even among the priesthood, miss that.
